document:
Kilo handles terminal input itself rather than relying on curses or another UI library. At startup the editor switches the terminal into raw mode through `enableRawMode`, which captures the current settings, registers a cleanup hook so they can be restored on exit, and applies a modified attribute set tuned for byte-by-byte interactive editing. The original settings are kept in a file-scope variable so the cleanup hook has them when the process ends. If raw mode has already been entered the function returns successfully without touching the terminal again. If standard input is not a TTY, or if the system calls for reading or writing the terminal attributes fail, the function takes a fatal path that sets `errno` and returns `-1`.

The attribute changes turn off every comfort the shell normally provides for line-based input. Echo, canonical line buffering, signal generation from keys like Ctrl-C, and extended input processing are all disabled, so each keypress arrives as a raw byte. Output post-processing is turned off so kilo can emit precise control sequences. Break handling, parity checking, eight-bit stripping, software flow control, and carriage-return rewriting are also switched off, and eight-bit characters are forced through the control flags. Read latency is tuned for an interactive editor: the terminal is configured so that a read can return after roughly a tenth of a second when no byte is available, rather than blocking. `editorReadKey` keeps polling for an initial byte and exits the editor if the underlying read reports an error.

When a byte arrives that begins an escape sequence, kilo decodes the trailing bytes and returns an internal soft key code rather than the raw escape characters. Bare escapes, the four arrow keys, page up and page down, home and end, and the delete key are all recognized through their standard VT100 escape sequences and mapped to kilo's own constants. Sequences kilo does not recognize fall back to a literal escape character.

Window sizing prefers the standard ioctl-based query. When that path does not yield a column count, kilo falls back to a cursor-query trick: it parks the cursor in the bottom-right corner, then `getCursorPosition` asks the terminal where the cursor ended up and parses the row and column out of the reply. `updateWindowSize` stores the resulting dimensions on the editor state and reserves two rows at the bottom of the terminal for kilo's status line and message area.

Window resizes are picked up live. The editor installs a `SIGWINCH` handler at startup. When the terminal changes size, the handler updates the stored dimensions, clamps the cursor if it now sits outside the screen, and refreshes the display so the user sees the new layout immediately.
